data.output:
1	---
2	title: Ecosystem
3	weight: 5
4	---
5	
6	DDx is one layer in a stack. Understanding where it fits helps you know what DDx does вҖ” and what to get elsewhere.
7	
8	## The Stack
9	
10	```
11	в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖпҝҪпҝҪв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
12	в”Ӯ  Workflow Tools                                      в”Ӯ
13	в”Ӯ  HELIX, your team's methodology, or custom workflows пҝҪпҝҪ
14	в”Ӯ  Opinionated: phases, gates, enforcement, practices  в”Ӯ
15	в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
16	в”Ӯ  DDx  вҶҗ you are here                                в”Ӯ
17	в”Ӯ  Shared infrastructure: document libraries, personas,в”Ӯ
18	в”Ӯ  bead tracker, agent dispatch, MCP server            в”Ӯ
19	в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖпҝҪпҝҪв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖпҝҪпҝҪв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
20	в”Ӯ  AI Agents                                           в”Ӯ
21	в”Ӯ  Claude, GPT, Gemini, local models                   в”Ӯ
22	в”Ӯ  Consume documents, produce implementations          в”Ӯ
23	в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖпҝҪпҝҪпҝҪв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
24	```
25	
26	## What Goes Where
27	
28	| Belongs in DDx | Belongs in a Workflow Tool |
29	|---------------|--------------------------|
30	| Bead tracker (work items, deps, claims) | Development phases and gates |
31	| Execution engine (define, run, record) | Phase enforcement and validation |
32	| Agent dispatch and token tracking | Supervisory loops and planning |
33	| Plugin registry (`ddx install`) | Methodology-specific skills |
34	| MCP server for beads and documents | Story/issue management beyond beads |
35	
36	## DDx Artifacts
37	
38	DDx produces two artifacts from a single repository:
39	
40	| Artifact | What It Does |
41	|----------|-------------|
42	| **`ddx` CLI** | Document management, bead tracker, agent dispatch, plugin registry |
43	| **`ddx-server`** | Serve documents, beads, and agent logs over HTTP and MCP |
44	
45	## Workflow Tools
46	
47	Workflow tools build on DDx's infrastructure to provide opinionated development practices.
48	
49	### HELIX
50	
51	A six-phase structured development methodology (Frame, Design, Test, Build, Deploy, Iterate) that uses DDx for document management, bead tracking, and agent dispatch.
52	
53	```bash
54	# Install HELIX with one command
55	ddx install helix
56	```
57	
58	{{< asciinema src="03-plugin-install" >}}
59	
60	Watch the full DDx + HELIX journey вҖ” from init to a working application:
61	
62	{{< asciinema src="06-full-journey" cols="100" rows="30" >}}
63	
64	[HELIX on GitHub вҶ’](https://github.com/easel/helix)
65	
66	### Your Methodology
67	
68	DDx is workflow-agnostic. You can build your own methodology on DDx's primitives, or use DDx without any workflow tool at all.
69	
70	## For Workflow Tool Authors
71	
72	If you're building a workflow tool on DDx, you get for free:
73	
74	- **Document library management** вҖ” your users already have structured docs
75	- **Bead tracker** вҖ” shared work-item storage with dependencies and coordination
76	- **Persona system** вҖ” bind agents to roles with predefined behavior
77	- **Agent dispatch** вҖ” invoke any supported AI agent through one interface
78	- **Plugin registry** вҖ” distribute your tool as a DDx plugin
79	- **MCP access** вҖ” agents can discover and read documents programmatically
80	
81	Focus your tool on what makes your methodology unique. Let DDx handle the infrastructure.
82

1	---
2	title: Plugins
3	weight: 6
4	---
5	
6	DDx plugins extend the platform with workflow methodologies, agent skills, CLI
7	tools, and library resources. HELIX is the reference plugin. You can create
8	your own.
9	
10	## What a Plugin Provides
11	
12	A plugin is a git repository that ships some combination of:
13	
14	| Artifact | Description | Install location |
15	|----------|-------------|-----------------|
16	| **Root** | The entire plugin tree | `.ddx/plugins/<name>/` |
17	| **Skills** | SKILL.md files agents discover as slash commands | `.agents/skills/` and `.claude/skills/` |
18	
19	All install outputs are project-local вҖ” DDx writes nothing under `~/`.
20	Skills are real-file copies, not symlinks. The project-local tree
21	(`.ddx/plugins/`, `.agents/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`) is committed to
22	git so teammates and CI get the same skills on clone.
23	
24	The plugin root at `.ddx/plugins/<name>/` can contain any DDx resource type:
25	
26	| Resource | Path in plugin | Description |
27	|----------|---------------|-------------|
28	| Prompts | `prompts/` | AI prompts and system instructions |
29	| Templates | `templates/` | Project and artifact templates |
30	| Patterns | `patterns/` | Reusable code patterns |
31	| Personas | `personas/` | AI persona definitions |
32	| MCP servers | `mcp-servers/` | MCP server configurations |
33	| Environments | `environments/` | Development environment configs |
34	| Tools | `tools/` | Tool configurations |
35	
36	This mirrors the DDx library structure (`.ddx/library/`). A plugin can ship
37	any subset вҖ” a persona-pack might only have `personas/`, while a full workflow
38	plugin like HELIX ships skills, a CLI, prompts, templates, and action docs.
39	
40	## How Installation Works
41	
42	When you run `ddx install <name>`:
43	
44	1. DDx fetches the latest release tarball from the plugin's GitHub repo
45	2. Extracts it to a temp directory
46	3. Copies the **Root** (the entire plugin) to `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`
47	4. Copies each skill from the plugin into `.agents/skills/` and
48	   `.claude/skills/` as real files (no symlinks)
49	5. Records the installation in the project's local install manifest
50

input.content:
---
title: Ecosystem
weight: 5
---

DDx ships in two layers. The platform provides primitives every document-driven
project needs; workflow methodologies layer opinions on top. Knowing which layer
owns which job tells you where to look вҖ” and what you should not expect from
DDx itself.

## Two Layers

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Workflow Methodology                                в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  HELIX, your team's process, or a custom workflow    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Opinions: phases, gates, supervisory loops, review  в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ  DDx Platform  вҶҗ primitives                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Document library, bead tracker, plugin registry,    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  agent dispatch, MCP server, git sync                в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

DDx is unopinionated about *how* you build software. It gives you the artifacts
agents read, the work items they claim, and the dispatch surface that runs
them. A workflow methodology decides what comes first, when a phase is
complete, and which review gates apply.

## What Goes Where

| DDx platform owns | A workflow methodology owns |
|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| Document library and templates | Which documents are required, and when |
| Bead tracker (work items, dependencies, claims) | Phase gates and definition-of-done |
| Plugin registry (`ddx install`) | Methodology-specific skills and prompts |
| Agent dispatch and harness routing | Supervisory loops and planning |
| MCP server for documents and beads | Story shape, review checklists, ceremonies |
| Git sync and execution evidence | Release process and deployment cadence |

If a question is "*how* should this team build?", it belongs in a workflow
methodology. If it is "*what* tools do I need to coordinate documents, work,
and agents?", it belongs in DDx.

## DDx Platform Artifacts

DDx produces two artifacts from a single repository:

| Artifact | What It Does |
|----------|-------------|
| **`ddx` CLI** | Document management, bead tracker, agent dispatch, plugin registry |
| **`ddx-server`** | Serve documents, beads, and agent logs over HTTP and MCP |

Both artifacts are workflow-agnostic. You can use them with HELIX, with another
methodology, or with no methodology at all.

## Plugin Registry

Plugins are how the ecosystem extends DDx. A plugin is a git repository that
ships any mix of skills, templates, prompts, patterns, MCP servers, or tool
configs. Installing one is a single command:

```bash
ddx install <plugin>
```

`ddx install` writes only inside the project вҖ” under `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`,
`.agents/skills/`, and `.claude/skills/`. The tree is committed to git, so
teammates and CI get the same plugin set on clone. Nothing is installed under
`~/`.

A plugin can be as small as a single skill or as large as a full methodology.
HELIX is the reference example, but the same mechanism distributes:

- **Workflow methodologies** вҖ” phase definitions, gates, supervisory skills
- **Skill packs** вҖ” agent slash-commands grouped by domain (review, testing, infra)
- **Template libraries** вҖ” opinionated starting points for new projects
- **MCP server bundles** вҖ” pre-wired integrations for agents to call

The registry is decentralized: any GitHub repo with a tagged release can act as
a plugin source. See the [plugins documentation](../plugins/) for layout and
authoring details.

## Agent Landscape

DDx is the dispatch and coordination layer for AI agents вҖ” it does not ship a
model. Beads, documents, and skills are consumed by agents you bring.

| Agent class | Examples | How DDx talks to it |
|-------------|----------|--------------------|
| Hosted frontier | Claude (Anthropic), GPT (OpenAI), Gemini (Google) | Provider SDKs via `ddx agent run` |
| Coding harnesses | Claude Code, Codex, Cursor agents | Harness adapters configured per project |
| Local / self-hosted | Ollama, llama.cpp, vLLM-served models | OpenAI-compatible endpoints |
| MCP clients | Any MCP-aware host | `ddx-server` exposes documents and beads |

Routing is configured per project in `.ddx/config.yaml`. The same bead can be
executed by a cheap local model, escalated to a frontier model on retry, or
reviewed by a different harness entirely вҖ” the bead tracker and document
library are stable across all of them.

## Workflow Methodologies

Workflow methodologies are layered on top of DDx as plugins. They turn the
platform's primitives into a development process.

### HELIX

HELIX is the reference workflow methodology: a six-phase structured process
(Frame, Design, Test, Build, Deploy, Iterate) with phase gates, supervisory
dispatch, and bounded actions. It uses DDx for document management, bead
tracking, and agent dispatch вҖ” and adds the opinions about *how* those
primitives are sequenced.

```bash
ddx install helix
```

{{< asciinema src="03-plugin-install" >}}

Watch the full DDx + HELIX journey вҖ” from init to a working application:

{{< asciinema src="06-full-journey" cols="100" rows="30" >}}

[HELIX on GitHub вҶ’](https://github.com/easel/helix)

### Your Methodology

Because DDx is workflow-agnostic, you can build your own methodology on its
primitives, package it as a plugin, and ship it through the same registry.
You can also use DDx without any workflow methodology at all вҖ” beads,
documents, and agents work fine on their own.

## For Workflow Authors

If you are building a workflow methodology on DDx, you get for free:

- **Document library management** вҖ” structured docs your users already have
- **Bead tracker** вҖ” shared work-item storage with dependencies and claims
- **Agent dispatch** вҖ” invoke any supported AI agent through one interface
- **Plugin registry** вҖ” distribute your methodology with `ddx install`
- **MCP access** вҖ” agents discover and read documents programmatically
- **Execution evidence** вҖ” every agent run is recorded for audit and replay

Focus your methodology on what makes it unique. Let DDx handle the platform.

input.command: git add website/content/docs/ecosystem/_index.md && git commit -m "docs(website): reframe /ecosystem/ as DDx platform + workflow methodology two-layer [ddx-dbeb3ddc]"
